name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًا كَرِيمًا (٣١)
```

```english
If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode. (31)
```

## (Commentary)

The verse is not lacking in connection to what preceded it, for the preceding verses contained mention of some major sins (المعاصي الكبيرة).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden) until His saying: **سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** (your misdeeds). Avoidance (الاجتناب) is originally derived from the side (الجنب), which is a physical limb; the verb was constructed from it as a metaphor (استعارة). This is because when a person desires something, he faces it with his countenance and the front of his body, but when he turns away from it and leaves it, he turns his side (جنبه) toward it and thus avoids it (اجتنبه). Therefore, avoidance (الاجتناب) is abandonment (الترك). Al-Raghib said: "It is more emphatic than abandonment," end quote. This is only because it is built upon a metaphor; from this same root come the terms side (الجانب), the led-animal (الجنيبة), and the stranger (الأجنبي).

Atonement (التكفير) is from concealment (الكفر), which means to cover (الستر). Its usage has become common in the Quran to refer to the pardoning of misdeeds. Major sins (الكبائر) is the plural of "major" (كبيرة), an adjective placed in the position of the noun it describes, such as "disobedient acts" (المعاصي) and the like. Greatness (الكبر) is a relative concept (معنى إضافي) that is only realized in comparison to smallness (صغر). From here, what is understood from His saying: **كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** (the major sins that you are forbidden) is that among the forbidden acts of disobedience, there are those that are minor (صغيرة). Thus, two things become clear from the verse: First, that acts of disobedience are of two types: minor and major. Second, that "misdeeds" (السيئات) in the verse refers to the minor sins (الصغائر) due to the indication of contrast (المقابلة) therein.

Indeed, disobedience (العصيان) and rebellion (التمرد), however they may be, are major and a grave matter when considering the weakness of the created subject (المربوب) in the presence of Allah, whose authority is magnificent. However, the comparison in this regard is between the human and his Lord, not between one act of disobedience and another. Thus, there is no contradiction between every act of disobedience being major in one respect, and some acts of disobedience being minor in another respect.

The greatness of a sin is only realized by the importance of the prohibition (النهي) against it when compared to the prohibition related to something else. His saying, the Exalted: **ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** (that which you are forbidden) is not devoid of an intimation or indication of this. The evidence for the importance of a prohibition is the intensification of the discourse through persistence therein, or the threat of punishment from the Fire, and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنُدْخِلْكُمْ مُدْخَلاً كَرِيماً** (and admit you to a noble entrance). Entrance (المدخل), with a *damma* on the *mim* and a *fatha* on the *kha*, is a noun of place (اسم مكان). What is meant by it is Paradise (الجنة) or the station of proximity (مقام القرب) to Allah, the Glorified, even if their ultimate reality is one.

## (A Discourse on Major and Minor Sins and the Atonement of Misdeeds)

There is no doubt that His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds) indicates the division of sins into major (كبائر) and minor (صغائر), the latter being called "misdeeds" (السيئات) in the verse. Similar to it in indication is His saying, the Exalted: **وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها** — **The Book will be set up. Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it. They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing, small or great, but has counted it!’** (18:49), as their apprehension of what is in the Book indicates that what is meant by "small" and "great" are minor and major sins.

As for the "misdeed" (السيئة), according to what its linguistic root and form provide, it is the event or action that carries unpleasantness (المساءة). Therefore, the term is sometimes applied to matters and calamities whose occurrence distresses a person, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ** — **and whatever misfortune befalls you is from yourself** (4:79), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ** — **They ask you to hasten the ill** (13:6). It is also sometimes applied to the results of sins and their external effects in this world and the Hereafter, such as His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَصابَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا** — **So the evils of what they had done visited them** (16:34), and His saying, the Exalted: **سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا** — **the evils of what they have earned will visit them** (39:51). In reality, this returns to the previous meaning. It is also sometimes applied to the sin itself, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** — **The requisition of an ill is an ill like it** (42:40). The "misdeed" (السيئة) in the sense of sin is sometimes applied to sins in general, encompassing both minor and major ones, as in His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ** — **Do those who commit vices suppose that We shall hold them as equal to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being the same? Evil is the judgment that they make!** (45:21), among other verses.

And it is sometimes applied to minor sins specifically, as in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds), for given the assumption of avoiding major sins, nothing remains for "misdeeds" except the minor ones.

In short, the indication of the verse regarding the division of sins into minor and major based on the comparison between the sins themselves is something that should not be doubted.

Likewise, there is no doubt that the verse is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان), and it strikes the ears of the believers with a subtle Divine care: that if they avoid some sins, He will atone for the others. This is not an incitement (إغراء) to commit minor sins, for that would be meaningless since the verse undoubtedly calls for the abandonment of major sins. Committing a minor sin from the perspective that it is minor—disregarding it and being negligent (التهاون) in its regard—becomes an instance of rebellion (الطغيان) and belittling the command of Allah, the Glorified, and this is among the greatest of major sins. Rather, the verse promises the atonement of misdeeds from the perspective that they are misdeeds that the human being—created in weakness and built upon ignorance—cannot avoid committing due to the prevalence of ignorance and passion over him. Thus, the course of this verse is like that of the verse calling to repentance (التوبة) which promises the forgiveness of sins, such as His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ** — **Say, ‘[Allah declares,] “O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.” Turn to your Lord...** (39:53-54). Just as it is not correct to say there that the verse incites to sin by opening the door of repentance and comforting souls thereby, so it is here. Rather, such addresses are a revival of despairing hearts with hope.

From this, it is known that the verse does not prevent the knowledge of major sins in the sense that the intent would be to guard against all sins for fear of falling into major ones; for that meaning is far from the course of the verse. Rather, what is understood from the verse is that the addressees know the major sins and distinguish these destructive sins (الموبقات) through the prohibitions related to them. At the very least, it can be said that the verse calls for the knowledge of major sins so that the legally responsible individuals (المكلفون) may be most concerned with guarding against them, without negligence regarding others, for such negligence, as you have known, is one of the destructive major sins.

This is because when a person knows the major sins, distinguishes them, and identifies them, he knows that they are sanctities (حرمات) whose violation cannot be overlooked through atonement except by definitive remorse and sincere repentance. This very knowledge is what causes a person to be mindful and to refrain from committing them.

As for intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — although it is a truth, you have already known from the previous discussions regarding it that it does not benefit one who belittles the command of Allah, the Glorified, and mocks repentance and remorse. Committing a sin while relying on intercession is a form of laxity and negligence regarding the command of Allah, the Glorified, and it is among the destructive major sins that definitively cut off the path to intercession.

From here, the meaning of what was previously mentioned becomes clear: that the greatness of a sin is known from the severity of the prohibition issued against it through persistence or the threat of punishment, as mentioned.

From the preceding discourse, the status of other views regarding the meaning of major sins becomes apparent, and they are many: Among them is what has been said: that a major sin is everything for which Allah has promised punishment in the Hereafter and for which a legal penalty (حد) has been set in this world. The flaw in this is that persistence (الإصرار) in a minor sin is a major sin, according to the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **لا كبيرة مع الاستغفار ، ولا صغيرة مع الإصرار** — "There is no major sin with seeking forgiveness, and no minor sin with persistence." This has been narrated by both groups (Shi'a and Sunni), despite no legal penalty being set for it in the Sharia. Likewise, the guardianship (ولاية) of disbelievers and consuming usury (الربا), even though they are among the major things forbidden in the Quran.

Among them is the saying of some: that a major sin is everything for which Allah has promised the Fire in the Quran; some added the Sunnah to this. The flaw in this is that there is no evidence for its converse being universally true.

Among them is the saying of some: that it is everything that intimates a belittling of the religion and a lack of concern for it; this was stated by Imam al-Haramayn and deemed good by al-Razi. The flaw in this is that this is the definition of rebellion (الطغيان) and transgression (الاعتداء), which is one of the major sins, yet there are destructive major sins even if not committed under this title, such as consuming the property of the orphan, incestuous adultery, and killing a faithful soul without right.

Among them is the saying of some: that a major sin is what is forbidden for its own sake, not due to an external factor; this is like the opposite of the previous view. The flaw in this is that rebellion, belittling, and the like are among the greatest of major sins, yet they are accidental titles (عناوين طارية), and by their occurrence in a sin and their attachment to it, it becomes one of the destructive major sins.

Among them is the saying of some: that the major sins are those contained in the verses of Surah al-Nisa from the beginning of the Surah until the completion of thirty verses, as if the intent was that His saying: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden) is an allusion to the sins explained in the verses preceding it, such as severing kinship ties, consuming the orphan's property, adultery, and the like. The flaw in this is that it contradicts the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse.

Among them is the saying of some (attributed to Ibn Abbas): everything that Allah has forbidden is a major sin, perhaps because disobeying Him, the Exalted, is a grave matter. The flaw in this is that you have known that the division of sin into major and minor is only by comparing some to others. What he mentioned is based on comparing the state of the human in his disobedience—he being a servant—to Allah, the Glorified—He being the Lord of all things. It is possible that some might lean toward this view by mistakenly assuming that the genitive construction (الإضافة) in His saying: **كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** is explanatory (بيانية), but this is false because the meaning of the verse would then return to our saying: "If you avoid all sins, We will atone for your misdeeds," and there is no misdeed with the avoidance of all sins. If the intent was the atonement of the misdeeds of the believers prior to the revelation of the verse, the verse would be restricted to those present at the time of revelation, which is contrary to the apparent generality (العموم) of the verse. Even if the verse were general, the meaning would return to: "If you resolve to avoid all sins and you do avoid them, We will atone for your misdeeds prior to that," and this is a rare, exceptional case or one that does not exist, which the generality of the verse cannot be carried upon, because the human species is not free from misdeeds and minor lapses (اللمم) except for those whom Allah has protected with His protection (عصمة); so understand that.

Among them: that a minor sin is that whose punishment is less than the reward of its doer, and a major sin is that whose punishment is greater than his reward; this is attributed to the Mu'tazilites. The flaw in this is that it is a matter not indicated by this verse nor any other verses of the Quran. Yes, it is established by the Quran that nullification (الحبط) exists in some sins in general, not in all of them, whether it is according to what they mentioned or not. The discussion on the meaning of nullification (الحبط) has already passed in detail in the second volume of this book.

They also said: it is mandatory to atone for misdeeds and minor sins when major sins are avoided, and it is not good to take one to task for them. This also is a matter that the verse does not indicate at all.

Among them: that greatness and smallness are two considerations that occur to every sin. Thus, a sin that a person commits out of belittling the command of Lordship, mockery, or lack of concern for it is major; yet that same sin, if committed due to a flare-up of anger, the prevalence of cowardice, or an eruption of lust, would be minor and forgiven on the condition of avoiding major sins.

Since these mentioned accidental titles are gathered by the concepts of obstinacy (العناد) and transgression (الاعتداء) against Allah, it is possible to summarize the discourse by saying that every one of the forbidden sins in the religion, if committed out of obstinacy and transgression, is major; otherwise, it is minor and forgiven on the condition of avoiding obstinacy and transgression.

Some said: In every misdeed and in every prohibition with which Allah addressed us, there is a major sin or major sins, and a minor sin or minor sins. The greatest of major sins in every sin is the lack of concern for the prohibition and the command and the respect for the legal obligation (التكليف). From this is persistence, for the one who persists in sin is neither respectful of nor concerned with the command and prohibition. Thus, Allah says: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ** meaning the major sins contained within everything you are forbidden, **نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** meaning We will atone for its minor part so We will not take you to task for it.

The flaw in this: that the necessity of every committed sin being accompanied by what makes it rebellion and self-exaltation over Allah, the Glorified—thereby becoming a major sin—does not necessitate that greatness revolves around this consideration alone, such that some sins would not be major in themselves without the occurrence of any of these titles. For incestuous adultery compared to looking at a stranger, and killing a forbidden soul unjustly compared to striking, are two major sins whether an accidental title occurred or not. Yes, whenever any of these destructive titles occur, the prohibition intensifies accordingly, the sin becomes greater, and the guilt becomes more magnificent; for adultery out of passion, the prevalence of lust, and ignorance is not like adultery out of deeming it permissible.

Furthermore, this meaning (that if you avoid the major aspects in every sin, We will atone for your minor ones) is a poor meaning that His saying: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** cannot bear according to its context, as is not hidden from anyone who has even a slight familiarity with the styles of speech.

Among them: what appears from the outward words of al-Ghazali, according to what is narrated from him (1), in combining the views. It is that among sins, by comparing some to others, there are major and minor ones, such as the adultery of a married woman from among one's kin compared to looking at a stranger; although some sins become major by the application of some destructive, ruinous titles to them, such as persistence in minor sins, whereby the sin becomes major after it was not.

Thus, it becomes clear that sins are divided into minor and major according to the comparison of some to others with respect to the act itself and the crime of the deed. Then, they are additionally divided into the two categories with respect to the effect of the sin and its bane in its nullification of reward by prevailing over it, or its reduction of it if it does not prevail, such that the sin vanishes with the vanishing of an amount of reward equivalent to it. For every act of obedience has a good effect on the soul that necessitates the raising of its station and its liberation from the filth of distance and the darkness of ignorance, just as every sin has an evil effect on it that necessitates the opposite: the degradation of its place and its fall into the abyss of distance and the darkness of ignorance.

So, if a person commits some sins while having prepared for his soul some light and purity through obedience, the darkness of the sin and the light of obedience must collide. If the darkness of the sin and the bane of the guilt prevail over the light of obedience and overcome it, they nullify it; and this is the major sin. If the obedience prevails with its light and purity, it removes the darkness of ignorance and the filth of the guilt by the cancellation of an amount of the light of obedience equivalent to the darkness of the guilt, and the remainder of its light and purity remains, by which the soul is enlightened and purified. This is the meaning of mutual nullification (التحابط), and it is the very same meaning as the forgiveness of minor sins and the atonement of misdeeds. This type of sin is the minor sin.

As for the equivalence of the misdeed and the good deed with respect to their punishment and reward, although it is something the intellect might entertain at first glance—the consequence of which would be the validity of assuming a "neutral" person who has neither obedience nor sin, and neither light for his soul nor darkness—it is invalidated by His saying, the Exalted: **فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ** — **A group will be in paradise and a group in the Blaze** (42:7). End of summary.

Al-Razi rejected this, stating that it is built upon the principles of the Mu'tazilites, which are false according to us. The author of *al-Manar* intensified the condemnation of al-Razi, saying:

"If this (meaning the division of sin into minor and major in itself) is explicit in the Quran, is it conceivable that its denial could be correctly attributed to Ibn Abbas? No, rather Abd al-Razzaq narrated from him that he was asked: 'Are the major sins seven?' He replied: 'They are closer to seventy.' And Ibn Jubayr narrated that he said: 'They are closer to seven hundred.' The view of denying the division of sins into minor and major was only attributed to the Ash'arites.

It seems that those among them who said this wanted to oppose the Mu'tazilites thereby, even if through interpretation (تأويل), as is known from the words of Ibn Furak, for he validated the words of the Ash'arites and said: 'The sins against Allah are all major; some are only called minor or major relatively (1).' The Mu'tazilites said: 'Sins are of two types: minor and major,' and this is not correct, end quote. And he interpreted the verse with a far-fetched interpretation.

Does one interpret the verses and hadiths just to oppose the Mu'tazilites, even in what they were correct about? That is not unlikely, for sectarian bias (التعصب للمذاهب) is what diverted many intelligent scholars from benefiting themselves and their nation with their intuition, and made their books a trial for the Muslims, occupying them with polemics therein away from the reality of the religion. You will see what al-Razi narrates from al-Ghazali and rejects for that reason; and where is al-Razi compared to al-Ghazali, and where is Mu'awiyah compared to Ali!" End quote. He refers at the end of his words to what we narrated from al-Ghazali and al-Razi.

__________________
(1) Meaning the relation according to the intentions of different sins, not the relation of some sins to others in themselves.

In any case, what al-Ghazali mentioned, although plausible in general, is not free from flaws in several respects.

First: That what he mentioned regarding the division of sins into minor and major according to the mutual nullification of reward and punishment does not always correspond to the division he mentioned according to the sins themselves and the core of the guilt at the beginning of his discourse. For most major sins that are accepted as such in themselves could coincide in their doer with a great reward that prevails over them; likewise, a minor sin could be assumed to coincide with the remaining reward in the soul that is smaller and less than it. Thereby, the minor and major would differ according to the two divisions: some sins would be minor according to the first division but major according to the second, and some would be the opposite. Thus, there is no total correspondence between the two divisions.

Second: That the collision between the effects of sins and acts of obedience, although established in general, is something that has never been established universally through the religious outward meanings (الظواهر الدينية) from the Book and the Sunnah. What evidence from the Book and the Sunnah indicates the realization of removal and nullification in a universal manner between the punishment of sins and the reward of acts of obedience?

That which has been detailed in the investigation regarding the noble, luminous states of the soul and the other base, dark states is also like this. For although they collide for the most part and remove and annihilate each other, that is not in a universal, permanent way. Rather, both the virtue and the vice may remain established in their place and reconcile upon staying, dividing the soul as if something of it belongs to virtue specifically and something to vice specifically. Thus, you see a Muslim man, for example, who consumes usury and does not hesitate to swallow people's wealth, nor does he listen to the plea of the destitute, oppressed debtor, yet he strives in the prescribed prayers and goes to extremes in his humility and submissiveness. Or he does not care about shedding blood, violating honors, and spreading corruption on earth, yet he is sincere to Allah with the utmost sincerity in matters of obedience and acts of proximity. This is what psychologists today call "split personality" after its multiplicity and conflict. It is when different psychological inclinations conflict and some revolt against others through crowding and contradiction, and the human remains in internal toil from that until the two traits (الملكتان) settle, pair up, and reconcile, each disappearing upon the appearance, rising, and seizing of its prey by the other, as you knew from the aforementioned example.

Third: That the consequence of what he mentioned is that the consideration of avoidance (الاجتناب) in the atonement of misdeeds would become redundant. For one who does not commit major sins—not because he restrains himself from them despite ability and psychological inclination toward them, but due to his lack of power over them and his inability to perform them—his misdeeds would be nullified by acts of obedience due to the prevalence of his reward, on the assumption, over his punishment, which is the atonement of misdeeds. Thus, no satisfactory reason remains for the consideration of avoiding major sins.

Al-Ghazali said in *al-Ihya*: "Avoiding a major sin only atones for a minor one if he avoids it despite ability and will, like one who is able to reach a woman and have intercourse with her but restrains himself from the act and limits himself to looking or touching. For the struggle of his soul (مجاهدة نفسه) in restraining from the act is more influential in enlightening his heart than his embarking on looking is in darkening it; this is the meaning of its atonement. If he were impotent, or his refrainment was only out of necessity due to inability, or he was able but refrained out of fear of a worldly matter, then this is not suitable for atonement at all. And everyone who does not desire wine by his nature, such that if it were permitted he would not drink it, his avoidance of it does not atone for the minor sins that are its preliminaries, such as listening to idle talk and stringed instruments. Yes, one who desires wine and listening to stringed instruments but restrains himself through struggle from the wine and lets himself go in the listening, his struggle of the soul in restraining might erase from his heart the darkness that rose to it from the sin of listening. All these are otherworldly rulings." End quote.

He also said in another place: "Every darkness that rises to the heart is only erased by a light that rises to it through a good deed that opposes it. Opposites are those that are proportionate; therefore, every misdeed should be erased by a good deed of its own kind so that it may oppose it. For whiteness is removed by blackness, not by heat and cold. This gradualness and precision is from the subtlety in the method of erasure; hope in it is more truthful and trust in it is greater than persisting in one type of worship, even if that is also influential in erasure." End of his words.

His words, as you see, indicate that what nullifies misdeeds is avoidance, which is restraint (الكف), even though it is not necessary according to this view.

The comprehensive discourse that can be said in this regard, supported by the noble verses, is that good deeds and misdeeds are mutually nullifying in general, but the effect of every misdeed on every good deed and vice versa in the manner of reducing it or annihilating it is something for which there is no evidence. This is indicated by considering the state of ethics and psychological states, which are the best aid in understanding these Quranic truths in the chapter of reward and punishment.

As for major and minor sins, the apparent meaning of the verse, as you have known, is that sins—by comparing some to others, such as killing a protected soul unjustly compared to looking at a stranger, and drinking wine by deeming it permissible compared to drinking it out of passion—some are major and some are minor, without this being clearly linked to the issue of nullification and atonement in its entirety.

Furthermore, the verse is apparent in that Allah, the Glorified, promises one who avoids major sins that He will atone for all his misdeeds, those that preceded and those that follow, according to the apparent absolute nature of the verse. It is well known that the apparent meaning of this avoidance is that every believer performs what he can of avoiding major sins and what is truly considered avoidance in his case, not that he avoids every single major sin by restraining from it. For one who pays the slightest attention to the series of major sins will not doubt that there does not exist anyone who inclines toward all of them and is capable of all of them, or it is so rare as to be non-existent. Interpreting the verse in this manner is not accepted by a sound disposition.

So the meaning is that whoever avoids those major sins he is capable of and which his soul yearns for—which are the major sins he is able to avoid—Allah will atone for his misdeeds, whether they are of the same kind or not.

As for whether this atonement is for the avoidance itself—such that avoidance is in itself an act of obedience that atones for misdeeds, just as repentance is—or that if a person does not commit major sins, he is left between his minor sins and his good acts of obedience, so the good deeds atone for his misdeeds—and Allah has said: **إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ** — **Indeed good deeds drive away misdeeds** (11:114)—the apparent meaning of the verse (**إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ**) is that avoidance has a role in the atonement. Otherwise, it would have been more appropriate to state that acts of obedience atone for misdeeds, as in His saying: **إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ** (Indeed good deeds...), or that Allah, the Glorified, forgives minor sins regardless, without the need to frame the speech as a conditional sentence.

The evidence for the greatness of a sin is the severity of the prohibition issued against it, or the promise of the Fire for it, or what is close to that, whether that is in the Book or the Sunnah, without evidence for restriction.

## (Narrative Discourse)

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The major sins are those for which Allah has made the Fire mandatory."

In *al-Faqih* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): regarding major sins, he said: "Everything for which Allah has promised the Fire."

In *Thawab al-A'mal*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Whoever avoids what Allah has promised the Fire for—if he is a believer—Allah will atone for his misdeeds and admit him to a noble entrance. The seven mandatory major sins are: killing a forbidden soul, being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين), consuming usury, returning to nomadism after migration (التعرب بعد الهجرة), slandering a chaste woman (قذف المحصنة), consuming the property of the orphan, and fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف)."

I say: The narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis regarding the enumeration of major sins are many; some will pass by you. Associating partners with Allah (الشرك بالله) has been counted among the seven major sins in what we will mention, except in this narration; perhaps he (peace be upon him) excluded it from among them because it is the greatest of major sins, as indicated by his saying: "if he is a believer."

In *al-Majma'*: Abd al-Azim ibn Abdillah al-Hasani narrated from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali, from his father Ali ibn Musa al-Rida, from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Amr ibn Ubayd al-Basri entered upon Abu Abdillah Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). When he offered salutations and sat down, he recited this verse: **الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَواحِشَ** — **Those who avoid major sins and indecencies** (42:37), then he stopped. Abu Abdillah said: 'What made you silent?' He said: 'I would like to know the major sins from the Book of Allah.' He said: 'Yes, O Amr. The greatest of major sins is associating partners with Allah (الشرك بالله), because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** — **Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him** (4:48), and He said: **مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْواهُ النَّارُ** — **Whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entrance to] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire** (5:72). After it is despairing of the spirit of Allah (اليأس من روح الله), because Allah says: **لا يَيْأَسُ مِنْ رَوْحِ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكافِرُونَ** — **no one despairs of Allah’s mercy except the faithless lot** (12:87). Then feeling secure from the scheme of Allah (الأمن من مكر الله), because Allah says: **فَلا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخاسرُونَ** — **No one feels safe from Allah’s stratagem except the people who are losers** (7:99). Among them is being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين), because Allah, the Exalted, made the undutiful one a tyrannical wretch in His saying: **وَبَرًّا بِوالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا** — **and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a self-willed wretch** (19:32). Among them is killing the soul that Allah has forbidden except by right, because He says: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ خالِداً فِيها** — **Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to abide in it [forever]** (4:93). And slandering chaste women (قذف المحصنات), because Allah says: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْغافِلاتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ لُعِنُوا فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخيرةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** — **Indeed those who accuse chaste, neglectful, faithful women shall be cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there shall be a great punishment for them** (24:23). And consuming the property of the orphan, because of His saying: **الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً** — **Those who consume the property of orphans unjustly** (4:10). And fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف), because Allah says: **وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَمَأْواهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** — **Whoever turns his back to them on such a day—unless maneuvering for battle or joining [another] troop—has certainly earned Allah’s wrath and his abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination** (8:16). And consuming usury (أكل الربا), because Allah says: **الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ الرِّبا لا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَقُومُ الَّذِي يَتَخَبَّطُهُ الشَّيْطانُ مِنَ الْمَسِّ** — **Those who exact usury will not stand except like one who has been confounded by Satan’s touch** (2:275), and He says: **فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا فَأْذَنُوا بِحَرْبٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** — **And if you do not, then be informed of a declaration of war from Allah and His Apostle** (2:279). And sorcery (السحر), because Allah says: **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا لَمَنِ اشْتَراهُ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخيرةِ مِنْ خَلاقٍ** — **and they certainly knew that anyone who buys it has no share in the Hereafter** (2:102). And adultery (الزنا), because Allah says: **وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثاماً يُضاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهاناً** — **and whoever does that shall encounter the reward of sin. The punishment shall be doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection, and he shall abide in it in humiliation** (25:68-69). And the false oath that immerses one in sin (اليمين الغموس), because Allah says: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعهدِ اللهِ وَأَيْمانِهِمْ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً أُولئِكَ لا خَلاقَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخيرةِ** — **Indeed those who sell Allah’s covenant and their oaths for a paltry price shall have no share in the Hereafter** (3:77). And misappropriating spoils (الغلول); Allah said: **وَمَنْ يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِما غَلَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** — **and whoever defrauds will bring his fraud on the Day of Resurrection** (3:161). And withholding the mandatory zakat (منع الزكاة المفروضة), because Allah says: **يَوْمَ يُحْمى عَلَيْها فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوى بِها جِباهُهُمْ وَجُنُوبُهُمْ وَظُهُورُهُمْ** — **on the day when these shall be heated in hell-fire and therewith their foreheads, their sides and their backs shall be branded** (9:35). And false testimony (شهادة الزور) and concealing testimony (كتمان الشهادة), because Allah says: **وَمَنْ يَكْتُمْها فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ** — **and whoever conceals it, his heart is indeed sinful** (2:283). And drinking wine (شرب الخمر), because Allah equated it with the worship of idols. And intentionally abandoning the prayer (ترك الصلاة متعمدا) or anything that Allah, the Exalted, has made mandatory—because the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) says: 'Whoever abandons the prayer intentionally has been released from the protection of Allah and the protection of His Messenger.' And breaking a covenant (نقض العهد) and severing kinship ties (قطيعة الرحم), because Allah says: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ** — **for such there shall be the curse, and they shall have the ill abode** (23:25)."

He said: "So Amr ibn Ubayd went out crying loudly and saying: 'Perished is he who spoke by his own opinion and contended with you in excellence and knowledge.'"

I say: Something close to it has been narrated from the paths of the Sunnis from Ibn Abbas, and two things become clear from the narration:

First: That a major sin among sins is that for which the prohibition is intensified, either by persistence and reaching the limit in prohibition, or by the promise of the Fire, from the Book or the Sunnah, as appears from the instances of his (peace be upon him) argumentation. From this, the meaning of what preceded in the (hadith of *al-Kafi*: "the major sin is what Allah has made the Fire mandatory for") and what preceded in the (hadith of *al-Faqih* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi: "the major sin is what Allah has promised the Fire for") becomes clear. The intent by making it mandatory and promising it is broader than explicit statement and allusion in the speech of Allah or the hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

I think that what was narrated regarding this from Ibn Abbas is also like this; his intent by the promise of the Fire is broader than explicit statement and allusion in the Quran or hadith. This is supported by what is in (*Tafsir al-Tabari*) from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The major sins are every sin that Allah concluded with Fire, wrath, curse, or punishment." Thereby it becomes clear that what was also narrated from him in (*Tafsir al-Tabari*) and elsewhere: "Everything that Allah forbade is a major sin," is not a disagreement regarding the meaning of major sin, but rather a magnifying of all sins by comparing the insignificance of the human to the greatness of his Lord, as mentioned.

Second: That the restriction of major sins to some of what preceded and what follows in the narrations—or to eight, or to nine as in some prophetic narrations narrated from the paths of the Sunnis, or to twenty as in this narration, or to seventy as in other narrations—all of that is in consideration of the different levels of greatness in sin, as indicated by what is in the narration of his saying when enumerating the major sins: "And the greatest of major sins is associating partners with Allah."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **اجتنبوا السبع الموبقات** — 'Avoid the seven destructive sins.' They said: 'And what are they, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'Associating partners with Allah, killing the soul that Allah has forbidden except by right, sorcery, consuming usury, consuming the property of the orphan, turning away on the day of the battlefield, and slandering chaste, neglectful, faithful women.'"

In it: Ibn Hayyan and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Bakr ibn Muhammad ibn Amr ibn Hazm, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wrote a letter to the people of Yemen—containing the mandatory duties, the sunnahs, and the blood-monies—and he sent it with Amr ibn Hazm.

He said: And in the letter was—that the greatest of major sins before Allah on the Day of Resurrection are: associating partners with Allah, killing a faithful soul without right, fleeing on the day of the battlefield, being undutiful to parents, slandering a chaste woman, learning sorcery, consuming usury, and consuming the property of the orphan."

In it: Abdullah ibn Ahmad narrated in *Zawa'id al-Zuhd*, from Anas: "I heard the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'Behold, my intercession is for the people of major sins from my nation,' then he recited this verse: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will atone for your misdeeds)."
